symptoms.symptom_name: Acute severe unilateral (left) otalgia with ear-tugging

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Acute unilateral otalgia with ear-tugging in a young child is a cardinal feature of AOM.
Otitis externa also causes unilateral ear pain but is distinguished by canal signs and pinna tenderness.

symptoms.symptom_name: Preceding viral URTI (rhinorrhoea, dry cough) for three days

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Antecedent viral URTI causing Eustachian tube dysfunction is the usual precursor of AOM in children.

symptoms.symptom_name: New conductive hearing loss on the affected side

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Middle-ear effusion produces conductive hearing loss, consistent with AOM.
Low-grade fever is common but non-specific; supports an infective process without discriminating site.

symptoms.symptom_name: No pinna/tragal tenderness and clear external canal (pertinent negative)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of pinna/tragal tenderness and a clear canal argues strongly against otitis externa.
Localises pathology to the middle ear rather than the external canal.

symptoms.symptom_name: Bulging, erythematous left tympanic membrane with loss of light reflex

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A bulging, erythematous tympanic membrane with lost light reflex on otoscopy is diagnostic of AOM — but requires otoscopy, impossible via telehealth chat.

symptoms.symptom_name: Reduced tympanic membrane mobility (pneumatic otoscopy / tympanometry)

investigation_details.expected_result: Reduced tympanic membrane mobility (Type B flat tympanogram) indicating middle-ear effusion
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Not required acutely — direct otoscopy is sufficient; tympanometry adds confirmation of effusion but is not needed to manage mild AOM.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Reduced mobility / flat tympanogram confirms a middle-ear effusion underlying AOM.

symptoms.symptom_name: No post-auricular swelling, redness or tenderness; auricle not displaced (pertinent negative)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of post-auricular signs and a non-displaced auricle is required to exclude mastoiditis; a caregiver can inspect and report the gross appearance, but definitive assessment is clinical.

symptoms.symptom_name: Sub-therapeutic single paracetamol dose; no ibuprofen; no regular analgesia

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Under-dosed analgesia explains ongoing distress and is the key modifiable management target, not a diagnostic discriminator.

symptoms.symptom_name: Alert, consolable, feeding with wet nappies (pertinent negative for systemic illness)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A consolable, feeding, alert child argues against serious systemic infection/meningitis, supporting an uncomplicated course.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Antecedent URTI + acute unilateral otalgia + conductive hearing loss + a bulging erythematous drum together constitute a high-probability uncomplicated AOM picture.
Negative post-auricular signs and a systemically well child exclude the principal AOM complications and justify community management with safety-netting.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person otoscopy (GP examination of the tympanic membrane)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Direct otoscopy confirms AOM and excludes alternative/complicated pathology; needed within 24-48 hours as telehealth cannot visualise the drum.
Not required acutely for mild AOM — direct otoscopy is sufficient; ordering it adds nothing to acute management.
Not indicated in an uncomplicated, systemically well child — over-investigation.
